articles.title: One of NYC’s Largest Residential Co-Ops Repositions Its 50K-SF Retail Portfolio
articles.narrative: Seward Park Cooperative, one of New York City's largest market-rate residential co-ops, has engaged Lee & Associates NYC to manage and reposition its extensive 50,000-square-foot retail portfolio in the Lower East Side. This strategic move aims to modernize the co-op's commercial holdings, capitalize on the neighborhood's evolving retail landscape, and reduce operational costs. The Lee & Associates team plans to attract larger brands and establish the portfolio as a destination, while continuing to service the local community.

data_points.notable_features: Portfolio of 25 retail properties within a 1,600-unit residential co-op, a rarity among Manhattan's large legacy co-ops.
data_points.project_notes: The co-op's board previously handled retail leasing in-house. The move to professional management is driven by the changing retail landscape and the desire to institutionalize the fragmented portfolio.

people.name: Wei-Li Tjong
people.title: President of the board of directors

articles.market_intelligence: New York City's retail market sustained strong leasing momentum in 2025, with availability hitting a 10-year low. However, the Lower East Side and East Village had a 15.43% retail vacancy rate in December, the second-highest among Manhattan's community districts.

articles.title: The Window for Seizing on New York’s Class B and C Office Rebound Is Closing
articles.narrative: The author argues that New York City's Class B and C office market has already passed its bottom, likely by six months, driven by the success of the 467m office-to-residential conversion tax abatement program. This program has led to the removal of approximately 26 million square feet of office inventory, significantly reducing oversupply. Concurrently, leasing activity has steadily strengthened, leading to positive absorption and a faster-than-expected approach to market equilibrium. The author notes a disconnect between improving market realities and lingering investor pessimism, suggesting that the window for buying at the bottom has closed and sophisticated investors are now recognizing the changed risk-reward equation.

articles.title: Capstone Equities Acquires 140 Crosby Street for $51M
articles.narrative: Capstone Equities, through its entity CE 140 Crosby Owner, has acquired the 35,000-square-foot office and retail building at 140 Crosby Street in SoHo, New York City, for $51.4 million. The acquisition follows Capstone's foreclosure proceedings against the previous co-owners, Madison Capital and Vornado Realty Trust, who had defaulted on the property's $75 million mortgage debt. Capstone had acquired the defaulted mortgage in July of last year, and the transaction was finalized this month with new financing from Prime Finance.

data_points.address: 140 Crosby Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Class A office building located in the heart of SoHo's retail corridor, bounded by East Houston and Prince streets.
data_points.project_notes: Previous co-owners Madison Capital and Vornado Realty Trust defaulted on a $75 million mortgage, which Capstone acquired in July last year. The property was previously marketed for sale at $100 million but failed to find a buyer.

articles.financing: The acquisition was financed with a $39.6 million agreement from Prime Finance, which consolidated prior mortgages and included a $5.53 million gap mortgage.

articles.title: Tanger’s Justin Stein On Leveraging Data and Gnoshes to Grow a Retail Portfolio
articles.narrative: Tanger, a prominent REIT specializing in open-air outlet and lifestyle centers, is experiencing a strong resurgence in retail, marked by 97% occupancy and 3.4 million square feet of leases executed over the past year. The company is actively pursuing growth through ground-up development, acquisitions, and strategic re-merchandising of its existing 41-center portfolio. A key part of their strategy involves diversifying beyond traditional apparel and footwear to include more food, beverage, entertainment, home, and health and wellness offerings, which has increased dwell time and sales. Tanger is leveraging data analytics to inform its leasing decisions and attract new brands, with many retailers planning their real estate needs several years in advance due to limited new supply in the market.

data_points.project_notes: Tanger has invested close to $1 billion in its portfolio over the past four to five years through renovations, external growth, and ground-up development.

people.name: Justin Stein
people.title: Executive Vice President and Chief Revenue Officer

articles.market_intelligence: There has been a lack of new retail supply added around the country, leading brands to consider markets they historically may not have, such as Huntsville, AL, and Little Rock, AR.

articles.title: Gary Barnett’s Extell Buys 165 East 56th Street for $39M
articles.narrative: Extell Development, led by Gary Barnett, has acquired the eight-story office building at 165 East 56th Street in Midtown for $39 million from the American Jewish Committee (AJC). The acquisition is part of Extell's ongoing strategy to assemble properties along the East 55th Street corridor for potential redevelopment, with the site marketed for office-to-residential conversion. The seller, AJC, had owned the property since 1959 and plans to relocate its offices.

data_points.address: 165 East 56th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Marketed as an opportunity for office-to-residential conversion, potentially eligible for tax abatements and a transit improvement bonus.
data_points.project_notes: The American Jewish Committee (AJC) had owned the property since 1959.

articles.title: Texas Retail Brokers Adjust To New Realities
articles.narrative: This article explores the evolving landscape for retail brokers in Texas, particularly those specializing in tenant representation, highlighting the increased complexities and extended timelines in securing retail leases. Despite a strong resurgence in brick-and-mortar retail, macroeconomic factors like high land and construction costs, coupled with elevated occupancy expenses, are creating significant challenges. Brokers are increasingly adopting advisory roles, educating tenants on market terms, setting realistic expectations, and assisting with due diligence, permits, and build-outs to navigate a tight market where landlords can be selective. The process from initial information gathering to store opening can now take two to four years, demanding greater effort and an anticipatory mindset from brokers.

people.name: Nick Naumann
people.title: Senior Vice President and Director of Brokerage

articles.title: JLL Negotiates Sale of 357,570 SF Distribution Center in Northwest Houston
articles.narrative: JLL has brokered the sale of Fairbanks Northwest Distribution Center, a 357,570-square-foot industrial property in northwest Houston. The two-building, cross-dock complex, completed in 2023, was 80 percent leased at the time of sale. The buyer was a fund backed by Ares Real Estate, acquiring the asset from seller Triten Real Estate Partners.

data_points.notable_features: Two-building, cross-dock complex with 32-foot clear heights, 72 dock doors, eight drive-in doors, and 10,471 square feet of office space.

articles.title: CenterPoint Acquires Two Industrial Buildings Totaling 192,318 SF in South Houston
articles.narrative: CenterPoint Investment Management has acquired two newly constructed, fully leased shallow-bay industrial buildings totaling 192,318 square feet in South Houston. The properties offer frontage along Beltway 8 and include a significant outdoor storage yard, appealing to industrial users seeking modern facilities with strategic access.

data_points.notable_features: The buildings feature 28-foot clear heights, a combined 52 dock-high doors, four drive-in doors, and a 1.3-acre outdoor storage yard, with frontage along Beltway 8.
data_points.project_notes: The buildings were fully leased at the time of sale.

articles.title: Partners Real Estate Brokers Sale of 114,525 SF Office Building in Northeast Dallas
articles.narrative: Partners Real Estate has brokered the sale of a 114,525-square-foot office building in northeast Dallas. The undisclosed buyer plans to implement capital improvements to the property. The seller and sales price were not disclosed.

data_points.notable_features: Seven-story building renovated in 2000.

articles.title: Taroko Sports to Open 11,740 SF Entertainment Venue in Pflugerville, Texas
articles.narrative: Taroko Sports, a baseball-themed entertainment concept, is set to open an 11,740-square-foot venue in Pflugerville, a northern suburb of Austin. This will be the company's second location in Texas and is scheduled to open early next year within the Stone Hill mixed-use development.

articles.title: Before you buy your first hotel: Key considerations for real estate Investors considering hotel acquisitions
articles.narrative: This article advises real estate investors, particularly those experienced with triple net leases, on the distinct considerations when acquiring hotels. It highlights that hotels are operating businesses within a real estate asset, requiring a different approach to valuation, due diligence, capital expenditures, and operational control compared to traditional net-lease investments. The author emphasizes the need for dynamic valuation methods, a focus on asset performance over tenant credit, careful modeling of recurring capital improvement plans, and strategic negotiation of management and franchise agreements.

articles.title: Trammell Crow Company breaks ground on 788,320-square-foot business center in Plainfield
articles.narrative: Trammell Crow Company has broken ground on Building 2 of the Plainfield Business Center, a 788,320-square-foot speculative warehouse in Plainfield, Illinois. This industrial facility is expected to be completed by the third quarter of 2027 and is being marketed for sale or lease. The broader Plainfield Business Center is planned to be a significant industrial development, ultimately comprising over 8 million square feet.

data_points.notable_features: Features 40-foot clear heights, 80 dock doors expandable to 156, and 211 trailer parking stalls. Located with proximity to Interstate 55 and I-80.
data_points.project_notes: Building 1, also 788,320 square feet, was delivered earlier in 2026 and is fully leased.

articles.title: Roseville’s Lupe Development Partners name president, COO
articles.narrative: Lupe Development Partners, a Roseville, Minnesota-based developer, has appointed Aaron Diederich as its new president and chief operating officer. Diederich, who previously served as vice president of construction, will now lead the company, which has developed over 2,200 apartment and condominium units and more than one million square feet of retail, residential, and industrial property since its founding in 1989. Former President Lucy Brown and former Vice President Steve Minn are retiring from full-time roles but will remain on the Board of Directors, with Brown as Executive Chairwoman, and will transition from their ownership roles over the next few years.

articles.title: Opus begins construction of Continental mixing facility in Mount Pleasant
articles.narrative: Opus has commenced construction on an 80,000-square-foot advanced manufacturing facility for ContiTech, a division of Continental, in Mount Pleasant, Iowa. The new compounding center will integrate production, warehouse, and office operations, including a three-story mixing facility, an automated high-bay warehouse, and office/lab space, all connected to the existing plant. Opus, acting as the design-builder, proposed a precast concrete system to meet the substantial load demands and improve efficiency for the specialized vertical manufacturing process.

data_points.notable_features: Includes a three-story 17,472-square-foot mixing facility, automated high-bay warehouse, and 4,250-square-foot office and lab space, all linked to an existing facility. Designed with a precast concrete system to accommodate substantial load demands for a specialized vertical manufacturing process.
data_points.project_notes: A portion of the mixing facility is expected to be ready by fall 2026 for the installation of critical mixing equipment.

articles.title: Newmark closes 72,745-square-foot industrial lease in ALgonquin
articles.narrative: Fromm International, a manufacturer of salon tools, has leased 72,745 square feet of industrial space at Algonquin Corporate Center in Algonquin, IL, relocating its national distribution operations from the O'Hare submarket. The Class A facility, developed by NorthPoint Development, offers modern industrial amenities and strategic access to I-90 and a skilled labor pool. Newmark represented the tenant, while CBRE and Stream Realty represented the landlord.

data_points.address: Algonquin Corporate Center Building 1, 3001 Broadsmore Dr, Algonquin, IL
data_points.notable_features: Class A facility with 36-foot clear heights, 130-foot truck courts, ESFR sprinkler systems, ample parking, and spec office space. Located with direct access to I-90.

articles.title: JPMorgan pulls plug on $1.4B real estate fund after years of losses
articles.narrative: JPMorgan Asset Management is liquidating its $1.4 billion US Real Estate Income and Growth Fund, a 24-year-old core-plus vehicle, due to years of deteriorating performance. The fund, which held a diversified portfolio of industrial, multifamily, and office properties, was particularly impacted by its office exposure amid higher interest rates, soft office demand, and a challenging transaction market. The liquidation process is anticipated to take up to three years, reflecting the current difficulty in aligning buyer and seller pricing expectations in the commercial real estate market.

data_points.property_name: JP Morgan US Real Estate Income and Growth Fund
data_points.notable_features: Portfolio split across industrial, multifamily, and office properties, with office accounting for approximately 16% of holdings.
data_points.project_notes: Fund held approximately $1.4 billion in assets at the end of 2025. Liquidation is expected to take up to three years.

articles.market_intelligence: Investor appetite for global real estate funds has started to rebound, with $172 billion raised last year, a 13% increase year-over-year. However, capital markets face challenges from elevated Treasury yields, high financing costs, and shaky transaction momentum. Commercial property sales saw a 25% year-over-year jump in the first quarter, but April volume subsequently fell 33% to $24.7 billion.

articles.title: Housing Notes: We co‑broke with everyone*
articles.narrative: Jonathan Miller, a prominent residential real estate analyst, has partnered with The Real Deal to publish his "Housing Notes" column and quarterly New York City market report. Miller's column discusses the ongoing challenges faced by Multiple Listing Services (MLSs) and the broader real estate industry due to antitrust litigation and evolving commission structures. He argues that while the Department of Justice and class-action lawsuits aimed to increase transparency and shift buyer agent commission payments, the market has largely maintained the status quo, with sellers still bearing the cost. Miller contends that the economic impact of these changes has been misunderstood, as buyers would simply offer less for a property if they had to pay their agent's commission out-of-pocket, ultimately not saving sellers money. He also highlights the contentious issue of private listings, particularly by Compass, which he views as unethical and detrimental to price discovery, creating a dilemma for traditional MLS systems.

articles.title: Slate Grocery REIT evaluates future after takeover offer
articles.narrative: Slate Grocery REIT is exploring strategic alternatives after receiving an unsolicited takeover bid from private affiliates of its external manager, Slate Asset Management. The Toronto-based REIT's board of trustees has formed a special committee to evaluate options aimed at maximizing unitholder value. The company's portfolio comprises 15.2 million square feet of grocery-anchored retail properties across 23 U.S. states, valued at $2.4 billion, with nearly half located in the Southeast.

companies_people.label: EQUITY/FINANCING
companies_people.firm_name: Teachers Insurance and Annuity Association of America-College Retirement Equities Fund (TIAA)

articles.market_intelligence: Grocery-anchored assets are perceived as safe investments in the retail real estate sector, contributing to a rise in consolidation among commercial real estate players.

articles.title: Kayne Anderson crushes goal with $5B real estate fundraise
articles.narrative: Kayne Anderson Real Estate has successfully closed its latest opportunistic real estate fund, Kayne Anderson Real Estate Partners VII LP, raising $5.12 billion. This significantly exceeded its initial $3 billion target and a $4 billion cap, marking the firm's largest real estate fundraise to date. The fund will strategically focus on investments in student housing, healthcare, and industrial properties, particularly last-mile logistics, with executives expressing strong conviction in the long-term demand and limited supply within these sectors.

data_points.project_notes: Fundraise exceeded original $3 billion target and $4 billion cap; executives contributed $65 million to the fund.

articles.market_intelligence: Student housing pre-leasing is outpacing last year's rates, according to Yardi Matrix data tracking 200 U.S. universities.

articles.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending May 22, 2026
articles.narrative: This article provides a roundup of the top construction permits filed in New York City during the week ending May 22, 2026, highlighting new building applications, major alterations, and demolitions. The largest new building permit was for a 105,697-square-foot residential project in Brownsville, planned to deliver 165 units. Other notable filings include a 91,404-square-foot residential building in Elmhurst and an 81,582-square-foot residential project in Gowanus. The largest alteration permit was for 316,832 square feet of space at 10 Astor Place in Noho, while the top demolition permit was for a 13,000-square-foot building in Fort George.

articles.title: PolicyPro: Unpacking Mamdani’s housing plan
articles.narrative: Mayor Zohran Mamdani has unveiled a comprehensive housing plan for New York City, focusing on expanding affordable housing, increasing tenant protections, and addressing distressed properties. Key initiatives include pushing for expanded project labor agreements in affordable housing developments, which has drawn criticism from some industry stakeholders concerned about potential cost increases. The plan also aims to intensify the use of the city's 7A program to take control of troubled buildings from landlords with chronic violations and introduce a new preservation program allowing distressed building owners to pool reserves for capital and operating needs. Additionally, the city seeks to facilitate the conversion of more rental buildings into co-ops and is backing two City Council bills: the SAFER Homes Act, which overhauls the Third Party Transfer program for distressed properties, and the Community Opportunity to Purchase Act (COPA), which gives city-certified nonprofits priority in acquiring certain distressed multifamily properties.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Small Property Owners of New York

articles.title: Danny Meyer heads to Hotel Bossert
articles.narrative: Union Square Hospitality Group, led by Danny Meyer, will open its first full-service Brooklyn restaurant at the historic Hotel Bossert in Brooklyn Heights. The restaurant will occupy 3,200 square feet on the ground floor of the landmarked building, which is currently being redeveloped into condominiums. The property was acquired by SomeraRoad last year for $100 million after a period of development challenges and a foreclosure auction.

data_points.notable_features: Historic landmarked building, formerly known as Brooklyn's Waldorf-Astoria.
data_points.project_notes: Building was previously acquired by SomeraRoad for $100 million last year after a foreclosure auction. Original plans by Chetrit Group and David Bistricer in 2013 included 78 guest rooms, a restaurant, and a rooftop bar.

articles.title: Jeff Sutton’s 599 Broadway value slashed by 80%
articles.narrative: The retail condominium portion of 599 Broadway in New York City, controlled by Jeff Sutton's Wharton Properties through a ground lease, has seen its valuation plummet by 80% to $32 million from $150 million in 2018. This sharp decline follows the loss of its anchor tenant and a lawsuit initiated by CMBS bondholders to foreclose on the $75 million mortgage, alleging a default on debt payments since January. The property's distress is further compounded by Wharton Properties ceasing rent payments to the building owner.

data_points.notable_features: Retail portion spans the basement and first three floors of a 12-story building.
data_points.project_notes: The retail condo was reappraised at $32 million, an 80% decline from its 2018 valuation of $150 million. The $75 million CMBS loan transferred to special servicing in January 2026. The ground lease with Wharton Properties was signed in 2008 for 49 years.

articles.market_intelligence: Competition for quality retail space in Soho remains intense, with median asking rents along the Broadway corridor reaching $750 per square foot, which is 12% below the 2017 peak of $850 per square foot.

articles.title: South Florida’s top deals: El-Ad buys Miami condo property for $46M
articles.narrative: El-Ad National Properties acquired Chateau Grove, a 25-unit condominium complex in Miami, for $45.5 million from an LLC managed by Jose Canero of The Canero Group. The 1960s-era property is located at 3265 Virginia Street. This transaction represents the top commercial deal recorded in South Florida during the period.

data_points.notable_features: 25-unit condominium complex dating to the 1960s.

articles.market_intelligence: U.S. home prices are showing mixed signals, with a national increase of 0.7% year-over-year in March, slightly down from 0.8% in February, while prices in over half of major metropolitan areas are declining towards pre-pandemic levels.

articles.title: Inside the latest in separate deadly incidents in Miami waters tied to real estate
articles.narrative: Two separate boating incidents in Miami waters, both involving prominent real estate figures and resulting in teenage deaths, are proceeding with different legal outcomes. Commercial broker George Pino, CEO of State Street Realty, faces a felony vessel homicide charge following a 2022 crash that killed a 17-year-old and severely injured another. His defense team is seeking to exclude evidence related to his alleged alcohol consumption and statements made before Miranda rights were read. In a separate 2024 incident, 15-year-old Ella Riley Adler, daughter of Matthew Adler of Adler Real Estate Partners, died after being struck by a boat while wakeboarding. The captains of both the towing yacht and the striking boat have accepted plea deals for misdemeanor charges, with the Adler family approving the outcome.

articles.title: “Calculated scheme”: Brickell Key condo owners sue Swire over $32M seawall and baywalk assessment
articles.narrative: Condominium associations on Brickell Key have filed a lawsuit against developer Swire Properties, alleging the company is illegally attempting to shift the financial burden of a $32.3 million seawall replacement and baywalk repair onto condo owners. The lawsuit claims that Swire, which developed the island, is violating state law by maintaining control of the master association for 44 years past the legal turnover threshold, and has used this control to improperly assess unit owners for work on property they do not own. The associations contend that the seawall is primarily owned by Swire and the City of Miami, and that the assessment was imposed without the required unit owner approval. Swire denies the allegations, stating it has not been served with the complaint and has always complied with all laws.

data_points.notable_features: Man-made island developed in the 1980s, home to approximately 11 condo buildings, retail, and an office complex.
data_points.project_notes: Lawsuit alleges developer improperly assessed condo owners $32.3 million for seawall replacement and baywalk repairs, claiming the developer has illegally maintained control of the master association for 44 years.

articles.title: Boucher Brothers, city of Miami Beach finalize $3M settlement with Nikki Beach operator
articles.narrative: A three-year legal dispute between the city of Miami Beach, Boucher Brothers, and the operator of Nikki Beach has concluded with a $3 million settlement. The lawsuit, filed by Penrod Brothers, alleged that city officials favored Boucher Brothers and Major Food Group's bid to redevelop the city-owned beachfront site at 1 Ocean Drive. The settlement clears the way for Boucher Brothers and Major Food Group to proceed with their plans for a new development, Boucher Brothers Pier Park, which will include multiple restaurants, a pool, retail, and other amenities.

data_points.notable_features: Redevelopment includes Mediterranean and Japanese restaurants, Sadelle's, beach concessions, a pool and retail pavilion, upgraded cabanas, a children's center, a wellness space, and public restrooms, with wheelchair accessibility to the public beach.
data_points.project_notes: The settlement includes a $3 million payment to Penrod Brothers, with Boucher Brothers paying $1.5 million directly and the city contributing $1.5 million through rent/concession offsets. Boucher Brothers received an extension to its development timeline and a five-year lease extension option. The joint venture has proposed a 10-year lease and concession agreement with the city valued at $41 million. Construction is expected to begin in summer 2027.

articles.title: Pending sale of McMackins’ $85M Manalapan estate tops signed contracts in Palm Beach
articles.narrative: A lake-to-ocean estate in Manalapan, featuring a unique underground tunnel, is under contract for $85 million, marking the highest-priced pending sale in Palm Beach County last week. The 2-acre property, built in 2020, includes a 22,900-square-foot mansion with seven bedrooms and 11.5 bathrooms, along with an air-conditioned beach bungalow. This transaction was part of a broader trend of 15 luxury properties, including 10 single-family homes and five condos, going under contract in the county, totaling nearly $210 million in asking dollar volume.

data_points.notable_features: Lake-to-ocean estate with an underground tunnel connecting the mansion to an air-conditioned beach bungalow.
data_points.project_notes: The sellers previously acquired the property for $38.9 million in 2020, the same year the house was built.

articles.title: Relu Stan’s Fulton Market hotel site returns to market with bonus air rights attached
articles.narrative: The site of an approved 296-key hotel project in Chicago's Fulton Market, previously home to Fulton Market Kitchen, has returned to the market for sale. Investor and developer Relu Stan is offering the property with an option to acquire additional air rights from an adjacent property managed by R2 and another Stan-owned building, potentially doubling the developable square footage to 151,000 square feet. The original 15-story hotel project approval is set to expire in January, with a one-year extension available.

data_points.notable_features: Site of an approved 15-story hotel project, with potential to double square footage via additional air rights.
data_points.project_notes: Original approval for the 15-story project is set to expire in January with a one-year extension available. The site was previously marketed in 2023 for $9.8 million under a sale-leaseback structure.

articles.title: Wicker Park Connection buildings list in West Town amid selling spree
articles.narrative: The Wicker Park Connection, a two-building mixed-use property in Chicago's West Town, has been listed for sale. Developed by Centrum Realty & Development's Arthur Slaven and John McLinden, the property includes 207 apartment units and 23,855 square feet of retail space, with a significant portion occupied by a major retailer. Brokers are highlighting the opportunity for continued rent growth in the area, as the listing comes amidst a surge in multifamily property sales in Chicagoland driven by strong rent growth and limited new construction.

data_points.address: 1640 and 1664 West Division Street, Chicago, IL
data_points.project_notes: The property was initially listed in 2018 but did not sell. It was refinanced in 2019 with a nearly $65 million loan from CBRE Capital Markets.

articles.market_intelligence: Chicagoland multifamily landlords are actively listing properties due to swelling rent growth and a lag in new construction. Chicago property sales have increased by 30% in the past year, outpacing national rates by 25%.

articles.title: Realty Income scoops up Chicagoland industrial center for $124 million
articles.narrative: Realty Income acquired a 910,800-square-foot industrial warehouse in University Park, IL, for $124 million. The property, fully leased to Central Steel & Wire, was sold by Affinius Capital and Venture One Real Estate. This transaction represents the largest single-property industrial sale in Chicagoland in the past five years, excluding data centers.

data_points.address: 23301 South Central Avenue, University Park, IL
data_points.project_notes: Central Steel & Wire secured a build-to-suit deal for this processing and distribution center in 2021.

articles.market_intelligence: This transaction is the largest industrial sector sale in Chicagoland in more than five years, not involving a data center. The last deal to exceed it was a $126.5 million purchase of a Michelin tire distribution center in Wilmington five years ago. Venture One Real Estate recently sold a 32-property portfolio for over $200 million to DRA Advisors and a 54-property portfolio for $369 million in 2023, with at least 40 properties in the Chicago area.

articles.title: GD Holdings buys 16 St. Regis Chicago condos from Sony for $21M
articles.narrative: GD Holdings has acquired a portfolio of 16 luxury condominiums, including penthouse units, within the St. Regis Chicago from Sony for $21 million. This transaction further solidifies GD Holdings' significant ownership stake in the Jeanne Gang-designed supertall, following a previous bulk purchase of 84 units from the original developer. Sony, an entertainment company, had only recently obtained these units from Dalian Wanda Group to settle an intellectual property dispute, indicating a strong motivation to quickly divest the real estate assets at a discount.

data_points.address: The St Regis Hotel, 401 E Wacker Dr, Chicago, IL
data_points.notable_features: Includes penthouse units on the 89th and 77th floors of the 101-story supertall tower.
data_points.project_notes: Sony acquired these units in January from Dalian Wanda Group to settle a $49 million intellectual property dispute. GD Holdings previously acquired 84 unsold units from Magellan in late 2024 for $117 million.

articles.market_intelligence: Penthouse units at the St. Regis Chicago are currently leasing for around $35,000 per month, while half-floor condo units command roughly $25,000 a month.

articles.title: Basis, Lessen put Loop office space on sublease market as vacancy crisis drags on
articles.narrative: Basis Technologies and Lessen are subleasing significant office space in Chicago's Loop, reflecting the ongoing challenges in the downtown commercial property market. Basis Technologies is offering its entire 70,000-square-foot space at the Sullivan Center, while Lessen is subleasing either 37,000 or 39,000 square feet across two floors at 203 North LaSalle Street. These moves highlight the trend of companies optimizing their office footprints in a remote-first environment and the broader market's flight to quality, with high vacancy rates persisting in both downtown and suburban Chicago.
articles.market_intelligence: The Loop office market is experiencing a flight to quality, with increasing demand for full-amenitized, top-tier office spaces. The pandemic's ripple effect continues to impact commercial properties, leading to high vacancy rates.

articles.title: Ricchi Group puts 15-story Dallas office on auction block with nearly $3M opening bid
articles.narrative: The Ricchi Group is auctioning off the 15-story, 268,000-square-foot office building at 1111 West Mockingbird Lane in Dallas, with an opening bid of $2.75 million. The property, formerly known as Regions Tower, is approximately 50 percent leased and sits on a six-acre parcel with significant development potential. Its strategic location near major highways and key Dallas destinations is highlighted as a notable feature.

data_points.notable_features: Located adjacent to I-35 East and Hwy 183, five minutes from Dallas Love Field and William P. Clements Jr. University Hospital, and 10 minutes from Dallas's central districts. The six-acre plot offers significant development potential.
data_points.project_notes: The building was previously acquired by an affiliate of Ricchi Group in 2012 for an undisclosed amount, with plans for a $3 million enhancement. The current county valuation for the building and land is upwards of $14 million.

articles.title: Austin’s St. Johns multifamily project to cost $58M
articles.narrative: Greystar is set to begin construction next month on the residential component of the St. Johns mixed-use project in Austin, TX. The multifamily development, comprising five buildings and 526 units, is estimated to cost $58.3 million. Over two-thirds of the units will be reserved for tenants earning below the area's median family income, reflecting a commitment to affordability in a market experiencing declining rents due to a supply boom.

data_points.address: 601 W St Johns Ave, Austin, TX
data_points.notable_features: More than two-thirds of units reserved for tenants earning below the area's median family income; includes retail space.
data_points.project_notes: The 19-acre site, formerly a Home Depot and Chrysler dealership, was acquired by the City of Austin in 2008 and 2013 using bond funds. The project will be divided into St. Johns South and St. Johns North, with the South Congress Public Facilities Corporation owning and ground leasing the parcels to Greystar. The project was halved to access more funding due to challenging market conditions.

articles.market_intelligence: Apartment rents in Austin have declined for 10 consecutive quarters following a supply boom during the pandemic. The city experienced a 4.8% year-over-year decline in average rent at the start of the year, the greatest drop among major metros.

articles.title: Harlingen city officials approve 120-day data center moratorium to modernize land regulations
articles.narrative: Harlingen city officials have approved a 120-day moratorium on data center development to evaluate land use regulations and assess the impact on public infrastructure, including electricity, wastewater, and water supply. This decision comes as the city faces a proposed 1,800-acre data center project by Eneus Energy just outside its limits, which city officials believe would strain current infrastructure. The moratorium aims to allow the city to modernize its regulations to accommodate future data center plans responsibly.

articles.title: Wells Fargo delivers boost to Austin proptech darling by lending for its 3D-printed homes
articles.narrative: Wells Fargo has partnered with Austin-based Icon Technology to offer mortgages for 3D-printed homes, addressing traditional lenders' reluctance due to concerns about the nascent technology. This initiative includes a 50 basis point credit for Icon home buyers who secure Wells Fargo mortgages, aiming to legitimize 3D-printed housing in the mainstream market. Wells Fargo is also financing the purchase of Icon's Titan multistory construction systems by other builders.

data_points.notable_features: Mortgages offered for 3D-printed homes, with a 50 basis point credit for buyers.
data_points.project_notes: Wells Fargo is also financing the purchase of Icon's Titan multistory construction systems by other builders.

articles.title: Another Sonoma County estate comes to market with local price record in crosshairs
articles.narrative: Two luxury estates in Sonoma County, "The Summit" and "Ghost Donkey Ranch," have been listed for sale, potentially setting new price records for the region. The Summit, a 1,119-acre property with a 10-bedroom home, is asking just under $40 million, while the 227-acre Ghost Donkey Ranch, featuring a 4,200-square-foot home and a working cattle ranch, is listed for $35 million. Both properties are poised to surpass the current county record of $30 million, set in 2010.

articles.title: South Bay mobile home parks trade hands in swath of deals
articles.narrative: Three mobile home parks in the South Bay area of California have recently traded hands for a combined total of $23.2 million. A group led by Mountain View developer Peter Wang acquired two properties, Mobilodge of Milpitas and Old Orchard Mobile Park in San Jose, for $9.1 million and $8.2 million respectively. Wang also sold the Mayfair Trailer Park in San Jose for $5.9 million to a Sacramento-based affiliate led by John McDougall of Monte Christo Communities. These transactions reflect continued investor interest in mobile home parks in the region.

articles.title: Ellis Partners, Baupost Group make priciest Silicon Valley office buy so far this year
articles.narrative: A joint venture between Ellis Partners and Baupost Group has acquired the Great America Commons office complex in Santa Clara, marking the largest office transaction in Silicon Valley so far this year. The two-building, 637,100-square-foot property traded for $310 million. This significant sale suggests a potential shift in the South Bay office market, which has seen a rebound in leasing activity during the first quarter.

data_points.address: 4555 and 4655 Great America Parkway, Santa Clara, CA
data_points.notable_features: Located next to California's Great America amusement park.
data_points.project_notes: The purchase price was 42.4 percent higher than the assessed value of $217.7 million in January 2025.

articles.market_intelligence: The sale is the largest office property transaction in Santa Clara County in 2026 and the second-largest in the Bay Area by dollar amount, following the $691.6 million sale of the Transamerica Pyramid in San Francisco in March.

articles.title: Gastropub Operator Chooses NoMad for Fifth Manhattan Location
articles.narrative: A gastropub operator has secured a 13,900-square-foot lease for its fifth Manhattan location at 373 Park Avenue South in the NoMad neighborhood. The space, formerly occupied by a long-standing restaurant, offers a significant footprint across the ground floor, lower level, and a mezzanine. This transaction highlights the ongoing demand from established food and beverage concepts for prominent, large-format restaurant spaces in Midtown South.

data_points.address: 373 Park Ave. South, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Second-generation restaurant space with ground floor, lower level, and mezzanine components.
data_points.project_notes: Space was formerly occupied by Dos Caminos for over 20 years.

articles.title: 2028 Olympics and Beyond: Talking with Exposition Park’s General Manager
articles.narrative: Exposition Park, a 160-acre state-owned campus in South Los Angeles, is preparing to host its third Olympic Games in 2028. General Manager Andrea Ambriz highlighted the park's extensive experience with large-scale events, including previous Olympiads, NASCAR, and major public gatherings, as a key asset in managing the upcoming Games. A significant focus is on transforming Los Angeles into a "transit-first" city for the event, which will require extensive public training and security protocols due to limited on-site parking and multiple activated venues. The park is also leveraging partnerships with local community organizations, leaders, and the city to balance the needs of the surrounding residential neighborhood with the international spotlight of the Games. Early and frequent conversations with stakeholders have been crucial, leading to a proposed $100 million in state funding for infrastructure improvements, including sidewalk and roadway repairs, to enhance accessibility and compliance.

data_points.notable_features: State-owned campus surrounded by a residential area in South Los Angeles; will be a multi-venue activated site for the 2028 Olympic Games.
data_points.project_notes: Infrastructure improvements are part of the California governor's proposed budget, focusing on major sidewalk and roadway repair, and accessible ramps for compliance.

articles.title: Centerbridge Commits to Two Floors at Rudin’s 345 Park Ave.
articles.narrative: Centerbridge Partners has committed to a 15-year lease for 75,826 square feet across two full floors at Rudin's 345 Park Avenue, establishing its new New York City headquarters. The private investment firm will relocate from its current nearby office in the second quarter of 2027. The landlord noted that the property offers a prime Plaza District location, best-in-class service, and an upcoming amenity suite, making it an ideal environment for leading companies.

data_points.address: 345 Park Ave., New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: 44-story Plaza District office tower with a top-tier amenity suite planned.
data_points.project_notes: Centerbridge will occupy the entire 22nd and 23rd floors and relocate from 375 Park Avenue in Q2 2027.

articles.title: $46M Bridge Loan Refinances Newly Built Moreno Valley Apartments
articles.narrative: Dwight Mortgage Trust has provided a $46 million bridge loan to Latco Enterprises for the refinancing of Villa Annette, a newly constructed 220-unit garden-style apartment community in Moreno Valley, CA. The financing will be used to retire existing construction debt, cover closing costs, and return equity to the borrower. The property offers convenient access to major regional transportation corridors, connecting residents to key employment centers across Southern California.

data_points.notable_features: Garden-style apartment community with 14 two- and three-story buildings, offering one-, two-, and three-bedroom apartments and community amenities. Positioned within the Inland Empire with direct access to State Route 60 and Interstate 215.
data_points.project_notes: Loan proceeds will retire existing construction debt, cover closing costs, and repatriate equity to the borrower.

articles.title: Well-Located Bergen County Multifamily Trades for $29M
articles.narrative: A 52-unit multifamily property located at 8 Tice Rd. in Franklin Lakes, NJ, has been sold for $29 million. Cushman & Wakefield represented both the seller, Adoni Property Group, and the buyer, Four Point Holdings, LLC, in the transaction. The property's modern amenities and strategic location in a desirable community, coupled with robust investor interest in well-located multifamily assets, contributed to a competitive sale process.

data_points.notable_features: Proximity to major roadways including I-287 and Route 208, offering convenient access to regional employment centers, retail destinations, top-rated schools, and community services.

articles.title: Canada Used To Send The U.S. Half Its Real Estate Money. No More
articles.narrative: Canadian institutional investment in U.S. commercial real estate has significantly declined, with transaction volume falling 32% year-over-year to $5 billion for the 12 months ending in March. This represents less than half the average annual investment from Canada over the past five years. The shift is attributed to political tensions, including tariffs and rhetoric from the White House, which have encouraged Canadian investors to diversify their global portfolios away from the U.S. While the U.S. remains an attractive market for its relative stability compared to other global regions, the push for diversification among Canadian investors is expected to be a lasting trend.

articles.title: Institutional Investors Are Following Each Other Back Into The Office Market
articles.narrative: Institutional investors and large publicly traded companies are re-entering the office market, driving a significant increase in investment sales volume. This shift is attributed to stabilizing fundamentals, including improved return-to-work efforts, increased leasing activity, and rising rents in trophy office spaces, making the asset class more appealing to risk-averse investment committees. The renewed confidence from major players like SL Green and Vornado is encouraging other institutional investors to follow suit, leading to both acquisitions and new trophy office developments in key markets.

people.name: Alex Ern
people.title: Senior Manager for U.S. Capital Markets, Market Intelligence

articles.title: 'Clicking On All Cylinders': Chicago Multifamily Sales Up 117%
articles.narrative: Chicago's commercial real estate investment market experienced a robust start to 2026, with overall sales volume increasing by 42.8% year-over-year to over $4 billion in Q1. This surge was primarily fueled by the multifamily sector, which saw a 116.6% increase in investment to approximately $1.9 billion, making Chicago the second-highest U.S. market for multifamily sales. The market is characterized by tight vacancy rates, limited new construction, and a perception of improving tax predictability under the incoming Cook County Assessor, Pat Hynes. These factors are making Chicago an attractive investment destination, with investors increasingly willing to overlook historical concerns about the tax environment due to strong revenue growth.
articles.market_intelligence: Chicago's multifamily vacancy rates are among the tightest in the country at 5.1%, trailing only New York and the San Francisco Bay Area. The city has just 1.6% of its current multifamily inventory under construction. Chicago has the lowest industrial vacancy rate of any of the top 15 metros.

articles.title: Canary Wharf's £1.5B BTR Bet Pays Off
articles.narrative: Canary Wharf Group's build-to-rent division, Vertus, has successfully established one of London's largest BTR platforms, nearing the completion of its build-out phase. Despite launching its first building, 10 George Street, in February 2020 at the onset of the pandemic, the portfolio now includes three stabilized properties and two new towers at 50-60 Charter Street, with another at 40 Charter Street completing in January. The residential expansion is a key part of Canary Wharf Group's strategy to diversify beyond corporate offices, with the BTR portfolio expected to total 2,445 units, contributing to nearly 4,000 homes built on the estate. The stabilized assets boast a 95% occupancy rate and a 60% tenant retention rate, attracting a diverse occupier profile beyond initial expectations of solely Canary Wharf workers.

data_points.notable_features: Amenities include a gym, reformer Pilates studio, private dining rooms, games room, cinema rooms, coworking facilities, and an outside terrace.
data_points.project_notes: The residential assets were valued at approximately $2.1B (£1.5B) by Brookfield in September. The portfolio also includes 972 residential units for sale and 615 affordable housing units, totaling about 4,000 homes built in less than eight years. Vertus Edit, a 378-room aparthotel, was pivoted from traditional BTR.

articles.market_intelligence: Rental growth has slowed to low single digits annually after a post-pandemic boom. The new Renters Rights Act, which removes fixed tenancies, has not impacted occupancy rates. The occupier profile shows 25-35% of residents work in Canary Wharf, a lower figure than initially expected, indicating success in attracting residents from other parts of London.

articles.title: Brookfield Sells Bethesda Tower For 87% Less Than It Paid In 2011
articles.narrative: In-Rel Properties has acquired the ground lease for 3 Bethesda Metro Center, a 388,000-square-foot office building in Bethesda, MD, from Brookfield for $20 million. This transaction represents an 87% discount from the $150.1 million Brookfield paid for the ground lease in 2011. In-Rel plans a substantial capital improvement program for the 52% leased property, including facade enhancements, a redesigned lobby, and new amenities, aiming to reposition it for modern tenants. The acquisition complements In-Rel's existing office building across the street, with the two properties now forming "In-Rel Plaza."

data_points.notable_features: Located next to the Bethesda Metro station.
data_points.project_notes: Brookfield previously acquired the ground lease in 2011 for $150.1 million. The Washington Metropolitan Transit Authority owns the ground. In-Rel Properties plans a substantial capital improvement program including facade enhancements, a redesigned lobby, a new fitness center, and a 100-person conference room.
